name: Defence & Trade
whatThisGrades: Federal action on defence spending and trade diversification away from U.S. reliance.
status: NATO 2.01% met. Trade diversification measurable: US share fell from 75.9% to 71.7%.
construct: The degree to which the federal government has met its stated defence spending commitments and diversified Canada's trade relationships away from single-market dependency.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: Defence target sustained and trade diversification materially improved away from single-market dependency.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: A- rather than A reflects Canada meeting but not materially exceeding the 2% defence floor, while trade diversification remains partly market-driven and the 3.5% target lacks a funded pathway.

activeModifiers.name: External Constraint
activeModifiers.reason: The trade war simultaneously creates the diversification problem and drives some of the measured improvement, so trade gains should not be treated as purely policy-caused.

up: 3.5% defence target gets a funded pathway
US export share drops below 68%

rationale: The government set a specific NATO target, funded it ($81.8B over 5 years), and delivered on timeline — NATO confirmed all allies met the 2% target for the first time in 2025 (Canada at 2.01% per NATO expenditure tables). Trade diversification produced measurable results: US export share fell 4.2 percentage points to 71.7% in 2025, the lowest since the early 1980s; non-US goods exports rose 17.2% to an all-time high (Global Affairs Canada). The A- rather than A reflects Canada meeting but not materially exceeding the 2% floor. Policy Options flags the 3.5% GDP defence target is unfunded long-term.

sources.label: Global Affairs Canada Monthly Trade Report (December 2025)

metrics.label: NATO spending (2025e)
metrics.sourceNote: NATO Defence Expenditures of NATO Countries (2014-2025), Table 3 (p.8) and Table 4 (p.9): Canada 2025e = 2.01% of GDP. Verified from Tier 1 PDF: nato.int/content/dam/nato/webready/documents/finance/def-exp-2025-en.pdf

metrics.label: US export share (2025 annual)
metrics.sourceNote: Global Affairs Canada Monthly Trade Report, December 2025: 'the U.S. share of Canadian goods exports fell 4.2 percentage points to 71.7% in 2025 compared to 2024, the lowest share since the early 1980s.' Based on StatsCan 12-10-0176-01.

metrics.label: Non-US exports (2025 annual)
metrics.sourceNote: Global Affairs Canada Monthly Trade Report, December 2025: 'annual goods exports to non-U.S. countries rose 17.2% to an all-time high.' Based on StatsCan 12-10-0176-01.

metrics.label: EU exports growth (2025 YTD)
metrics.sourceNote: Global Affairs Canada Monthly Trade Report, December 2025: EU goods exports +23.4% year-to-date. Substituted for prior unverified '$134B CETA trade' figure in March 2026 cycle. Canonical-Scoring-Sheets.md and Pilot-Templates.md updated to reflect this substitution.

promises.evidence: Confirmed by NATO Secretary General annual report. $81.8B committed over five years.
One Canadian Economy Act (Bill C-5) received Royal Assent June 26, 2025 with bipartisan support.
promises.statusSourceLabel: Government of Canada — Internal Trade Action Plan status

promises.evidence: Announced in Budget 2025. Fund structure being designed. No disbursements yet.
Policy framework announced. Implementation underway across departments.
Announced but not yet launched. No timeline confirmed.

perspectives.critics: Policy Options: the 3.5% GDP defence target cannot be financed without tax increases or structural deficits. NDP and labour groups: $81.8B for defence while housing, healthcare, and childcare remain underfunded is a priorities failure, not a win.
perspectives.defenders: Fraser Institute and NATO allies confirm this is the most significant Canadian defence achievement since the Cold War. Trade diversification is measurable: US export share fell from 75.9% (2024) to 71.7% (2025), and non-US goods exports rose 17.2% to an all-time high (Global Affairs Canada).

nextTrigger: NATO annual defence spending report (June 2026). CUSMA review (July 2026). Next trade data release.
inherited: Canada had not met NATO 2% since the Cold War. Trade was 75.9% US-dependent in 2024 (Global Affairs Canada, December 2025 Monthly Trade Report). Interprovincial barriers were decades-old.

defence.label: Defence
defence.rationale: NATO 2% met and confirmed by NATO Secretary General Annual Report. $81.8B committed. Procurement advancing.

trade.label: Trade Diversification
trade.rationale: US export share fell 4.2 percentage points to 71.7% in 2025, the lowest since the early 1980s (Global Affairs Canada, December 2025 report). Non-US goods exports rose 17.2% to an all-time high. EU exports up 23.4% YTD. January 2026 monthly showed weakness (-6.5%) but single-month data is volatile.

name: Major Projects
whatThisGrades: How well the federal machinery for approving and delivering major infrastructure is working.
status: MPO created with real powers. Credit-claiming exceeds contribution. National interest designation unused.
construct: The effectiveness of federal institutional machinery for infrastructure project delivery.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: MPO created but most projects are pre-existing, the signature power is unused, and the framework remains untested.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: Current C includes a credit-claiming penalty. Without that documented penalty for overstating ownership of pre-existing projects, the raw assessment would sit at C+.

activeModifiers.name: Credit-claiming penalty
activeModifiers.reason: Most projects in the pipeline were pre-existing, and documented overclaiming pulls the raw assessment down from C+ to C.

activeModifiers.name: Timing Fairness
activeModifiers.reason: Infrastructure is multi-year and the MPO is still young, but the machinery itself is already gradeable.

up: First use of national interest designation
First project completes a full MPO cycle
down: Evidence that the framework is being bypassed

rationale: The Major Projects Office is a real institutional innovation with potentially transformative powers (Building Canada Act, C-5). However, the $116B headline figure primarily represents pre-existing investment decisions. The national interest designation has never been used. The Fraser Institute and Angus Reid both found the government overstated its role. AI investment and northern strategy are too early to judge. Note: the credit-claiming penalty modifier (-0.3 GPA) is reflected in the current C grade. Without the penalty, the raw assessment would be C+, reduced to C based on documented overclaiming of pre-existing projects by Fraser Institute and Angus Reid.

metrics.label: MPO project tranches announced
metrics.sourceNote: Two tranches of nation-building projects referred to the Major Projects Office. Source: PMO announcements (see dimension sources). Specific project lists are in the PMO releases.

metrics.label: Headline investment figure (government-announced)
metrics.sourceNote: PMO nation-building project announcements (see dimension sources). $116B is the government's stated total across both tranches. Fraser Institute and Angus Reid both found the figure primarily represents pre-existing investment decisions — qualifier 'mostly pre-existing' reflects their assessment.

metrics.label: National interest designation uses
metrics.sourceNote: Building Canada Act grants the national interest designation power. As of March 2026, it has not been used. Source: no designation orders found in LEGISinfo or government announcements.

metrics.label: Bills passed
metrics.sourceNote: LEGISinfo (parl.ca/legisinfo): Building Canada Act (Bill C-5) passed. Source URL in dimension sources array.

promises.text: Major Federal Project Office with 2-year decisions
promises.evidence: MPO created and operational. No project has completed the full 2-year cycle yet.

promises.text: One Project, One Review within 6 months
promises.evidence: Aspiration stated but regulatory harmonization not yet achieved across agencies.
promises.statusSourceLabel: Bill C-5 — One Canadian Economy Act on LEGISinfo

promises.evidence: Alto concept announced. No construction expected before 2029.
Program expanded to $10B. Operational and accepting applications.

perspectives.critics: Angus Reid and Fraser Institute: the $116B headline is mostly pre-existing investment rebranded. The national interest designation has never been used — the MPO's most powerful tool sits on the shelf. Environmental and Indigenous groups: fast-tracking without consent is not progress.
perspectives.defenders: The Building Canada Act is genuine institutional reform with bipartisan support. The $10B Indigenous Loan Guarantee is operational. The MPO is less than a year old — judging it on unused powers rather than institutional capacity is premature.

nextTrigger: First use of national interest designation. MPO project completion or cancellation. Alto rail feasibility report.
inherited: Regulatory assessments routinely exceeded 5 years. Bill C-69 was criticized across the spectrum. Private investment proceeded despite, not because of, the framework.

name: Fiscal Health
whatThisGrades: Federal fiscal trajectory — is the spending and deficit path sustainable and credible?
status: Deficit $78.3B projected. No credible medium-term anchor. PBO gives 7.5% chance of meeting targets.
construct: The sustainability of the federal fiscal trajectory and the credibility of the medium-term fiscal plan.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: Deficit remains above a sustainable range, the medium-term anchor is not credible, and independent confidence in the fiscal path is low.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: Current D reflects a large projected deficit, only 7.5% PBO confidence in the stated path, and rating pressure warnings despite strategically justified defence spending.

activeModifiers.name: External Constraint
activeModifiers.reason: Trade-war-era defence spending is strategically justified, but it does not excuse broader discretionary fiscal expansion such as the gas tax suspension.

up: PBO confidence rises above 25%
Fitch removes its warning

rationale: The projected deficit of $78.3B (FY 2025-26, Budget 2025 Annex 1) represents a substantial increase from the prior year. Fitch maintained its AA+ rating with a stable outlook after Budget 2025 but warned that persistent fiscal expansion could increase rating pressure over the medium term. The PBO gives only 7.5% probability the deficit-to-GDP ratio will decline as projected. Defence spending is strategically justified, but the broader fiscal package lacks a credible anchor. GST revenue ($54.4B) is now exceeded by debt servicing ($55.6B) — both figures verified from Budget 2025 Annex 1. IMF/IFSD note current debt levels are manageable, but the trajectory given defence + capital commitments is unsustainable without tax increases.

metrics.label: Deficit (FY 2025-26, projected)
metrics.sourceNote: Budget 2025 Annex 1, Table A1.7: budgetary balance -$78.3 billion. This is a budget projection, not an actual outturn.

metrics.label: Federal debt (2024-25)
metrics.sourceNote: Federal debt (accumulated deficit) $1,266.5B per Annual Financial Report FY 2024-25. Note: Budget 2025 'net debt' (different concept, includes non-financial assets) is $1,480.4B for FY 2025-26.

metrics.label: Fitch rating
metrics.sourceNote: Fitch maintained AA+/stable after Budget 2025 (Nov 2025). Fitch warned that persistent fiscal expansion and rising debt burden 'could increase rating pressure over the medium term' but did not change the outlook to negative. Sources: The Deep Dive (thedeepdive.ca/liberal-victory-triggers-fiscal-warning-from-fitch), Yahoo Finance Canada (ca.finance.yahoo.com/news/heres-only-credit-rating-agency-123856595.html). Event-driven — next check on any Fitch action or statement.

promises.evidence: No credible path identified. PBO projects 7.5% probability of meeting deficit targets.
Spending review announced. Departmental cuts being implemented. Broadbent Institute warns of service delivery impacts.
Implemented in Budget 2025.
Reversed in Budget 2025. Fraser Institute acknowledged as step in right direction.
promises.statusSourceLabel: Department of Finance — capital gains inclusion rate announcement

perspectives.critics: C.D. Howe: every GST dollar now goes to debt servicing. PBO gives 7.5% probability of meeting deficit targets — in any other context that plan gets rejected. Fitch maintained AA+ but warned persistent deficits could increase rating pressure. La Presse: the gas tax suspension costs $2.4B the government cannot afford.
perspectives.defenders: IMF and IFSD note Canada's debt-to-GDP ratio remains manageable by international standards. Defence spending is strategically necessary during a trade war. The operating/capital budget split is a legitimate framework innovation. Kevin Page: "There is no fiscal crisis."

nextTrigger: April 28, 2026 economic update from Finance Minister Champagne. PBO spring fiscal outlook. Fitch/Moody's review.
inherited: Elevated post-pandemic deficits. Rising debt-servicing costs. Fiscal anchor of reducing debt-to-GDP already abandoned by prior government.

name: Economic Policy Response
whatThisGrades: Federal action on Canada's productivity and competitiveness — investment, innovation, jobs, wages.
status: Structural productivity crisis inherited. AI and minerals policies are directionally correct, but execution evidence remains thinner than the announcements and business investment is still flat. Grade reflects policy response and trajectory, not inherited conditions.
construct: The adequacy of the federal government's policy response to Canada's structural productivity and competitiveness challenges.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: Productivity instruments are directionally correct but not yet executing strongly enough, while business investment remains flat or declining.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: Current D reflects a real policy response that has not yet produced measurable traction: key funds are still more announced than executed, business investment remains flat, and major domestic barriers remain unresolved.

activeModifiers.name: Timing Fairness
activeModifiers.reason: This is a long-lag file, so the grade assesses trajectory and adequacy of response rather than inherited GDP per capita outcomes.

activeModifiers.name: External Constraint
activeModifiers.reason: The trade war constrains business investment decisions, but it does not resolve the domestic productivity barriers that still sit inside federal control.

up: AI compute fund begins actual disbursement confirmed by Treasury Board or PBO
Business investment turns positive for two or more consecutive quarters
down: Additional major fund announced without Treasury Board authorization

rationale: This dimension grades the government's policy response to Canada's structural productivity and competitiveness challenges — not the inherited GDP per capita level, which reflects a decade of underinvestment. The AI compute strategy ($2B), critical minerals push (still primarily government-stated rather than authorization/disbursement-backed in the live stack), and interprovincial reform are directionally correct. But business investment remains flat for a fourth year, specific barriers (permitting, tax rates, electricity costs) remain unresolved, and OECD long-term projections rank Canada last among 38 members for per-capita growth. The grade reflects whether the policy direction and scale are adequate to change the trajectory, recognizing that outcomes will take years to materialize.

metrics.label: OECD per-capita growth rank (2020-2030 forecast)
metrics.sourceNote: OECD long-term baseline projections remain the primary reference point; OECD Economic Surveys: Canada 2025 summarizes the weak per-capita outlook, while Fraser Institute and BCBC analyses map those baseline projections into the 'last or near-last' framing used here. Canada's real GDP per capita is forecast at roughly 0.7%/yr in 2020-2030 and 0.8%/yr in 2030-2060. Note: ranking is 'last or near-last' depending on projection vintage; tied with Italy in some versions.

promises.evidence: Launched Dec 2024. Microsoft committed C$19B. SCALE AI $128.5M round completed.

NRCan states $18.5B mobilized across Canadian critical minerals projects (see dimension sources; March 2026 NRCan release consolidates the March and October rounds). The '56 partnerships' figure refers to CMGD internal R&D projects, not the $18.5B total. This is policy/context evidence, not proof of federal disbursement. Canada produces 10 of 12 NATO defence-critical minerals.
promises.statusSourceLabel: Government of Canada — Critical Minerals Strategy

promises.evidence: Announced but reports not yet public.

perspectives.critics: If Carney is the credibility candidate — the one markets trust — why has business investment not turned after 13 months? Permitting, tax rates, electricity costs, and regulatory uncertainty remain unresolved. OECD long-term projections rank Canada last or near-last among 38 members for per-capita GDP growth through 2060 (Fraser Institute / BCBC analysis of OECD data).
perspectives.defenders: The AI compute strategy ($2B), critical minerals ($18.5B), and Microsoft's $19B commitment are directionally correct. Productivity is a decade-long structural problem — expecting a 13-month turnaround misunderstands the timeline required.

nextTrigger: Treasury Board authorization or first confirmed disbursement for AI compute. Q1 2026 business investment data. Any authorization or disbursement evidence for critical minerals instruments.
inherited: GDP per capita declined 2.0% from 2020-2024. Business investment falling since 2006. R&D at half the US rate. Population-driven growth masked per-capita decline for a decade.

name: Affordability Response
whatThisGrades: Federal response to household cost pressure — groceries, tariff-driven costs, targeted relief.
status: Government affordability measures cover <20% of household cost increases. Grocery Code voluntary. Tariff costs add $1,450-$2,000/yr per household. Grade reflects federal policy response, not global price levels.
construct: Adequacy of the federal policy response to household cost pressure from groceries, tariffs, and targeted relief.
rationale: This dimension grades the adequacy of the federal government's policy response to household cost pressure — not the price levels themselves, which are driven by global markets, exchange rates, and supply chains largely outside federal control. The $307 grocery benefit covers less than 20% of the estimated annual cost increase. The Grocery Code of Conduct is voluntary. Retaliatory tariffs compound the burden. The grade reflects what the government chose to do about affordability, and whether its interventions match the scale of the problem.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: Federal relief covers less than 20% of estimated annual cost increase.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: D- per rubric: near-total failure; relief covers a negligible share of the identified cost and structural drivers remain unaddressed.

activeModifiers.name: External Constraint
activeModifiers.reason: Tariffs add $1,450-$2,000/yr per household. Per rubric condition, the modifier applies only when the government's response is adequate given the constraint. Response judged inadequate, so the modifier was not applied.

up: New federal benefit >$500/household announced and funded
Mandatory grocery competition measure enacted

metrics.label: Food CPI (stores, YoY, Feb 2026)
metrics.sourceNote: StatsCan CPI daily release, March 16, 2026: 'Food purchased from stores rose 4.1% in February following a 4.8% increase in January.' Prior dashboard value of 5.4% was incorrect — corrected to match verified source.

promises.evidence: GST/HST Credit top-up operational. PBO estimates ~$307 incremental per household. Full benefit up to $1,890 for family of four.
Operational Jan 1, 2026. Voluntary framework. Experts broadly aligned it will not lower retail prices.
Policy framework announced. Implementation details pending.

perspectives.critics: NDP: $307 per household against $1,600+ in cost increases is an insult to struggling families. The Grocery Code is voluntary by design — the government chose not to regulate a concentrated industry. Broadbent: this is a wealth transfer from consumers to oligopolies.
perspectives.defenders: Global food markets, exchange rates, and retaliatory tariffs drive most of the price increase — these are largely outside federal control. The benefit reaches the lowest-income households first.

nextTrigger: Next monthly CPI release. Any new grocery benefit or mandatory grocery competition measure. Next PROOF food insecurity release.
inherited: Prices already 30%+ above pre-pandemic levels. Highly concentrated retail sector. Competition Bureau investigations underway.

name: Carbon Pricing Policy
whatThisGrades: Federal carbon pricing policy — consumer charge changes and the industrial system.
status: Consumer carbon price eliminated (affordability win). Industrial OBPS preserved at $95/t headline but ~$20/t effective. Grade assesses the pricing instrument only; the wider climate framework is graded under Climate & Environment.
construct: The quality of the federal government's handling of carbon pricing as a policy instrument.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: One instrument was delivered, but the remaining industrial instrument is preserved more in form than in clearly effective function.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: Current C reflects a genuine consumer-policy delivery win alongside an industrial OBPS whose effective price remains far below its headline design and therefore invites doubts about functional coherence.

activeModifiers.name: External Constraint
activeModifiers.reason: A US border carbon adjustment could eventually justify tighter industrial pricing, but that proposal is still too early to apply as a modifier.

up: OBPS tightened with effective price rising above $40/t
Formal carbon border adjustment mechanism announced with implementation plan
down: OBPS further weakened with effective price below $15/t
Government announces intention to eliminate industrial pricing

rationale: This dimension grades the government's handling of carbon pricing as a policy instrument — the decision to eliminate the consumer levy, preserve the industrial system, and the coherence of that distinction. The consumer elimination was a genuine affordability win and Day 1 commitment delivered. The industrial system's preservation is defensible on competitiveness grounds. However, CCI and IISD both note that credit oversupply can pull the effective compliance price far below the $95 headline schedule, raising questions about whether the industrial system achieves its stated purpose. The grade reflects policy handling quality, not climate outcomes (which are graded under Climate & Environment).

sources.label: IISD — Canadian Carbon Pricing Systems: 2025 Review

metrics.label: Consumer carbon tax
metrics.sourceNote: Finance Canada: fuel charge rate set to zero effective April 1, 2025. Source: canada.ca/en/department-finance/news/2025/03/removing-the-consumer-carbon-price-effective-april-1-2025.html

metrics.label: Industrial OBPS (headline rate)
metrics.sourceNote: ECCC Output-Based Pricing System: excess emissions charge $95/tCO2e in 2025, increasing $15/yr to $170 by 2030. CCI and IISD both note that credit oversupply can pull the effective compliance price materially below the headline schedule. Headline and effective rates are different measures — dashboard shows the headline rate; rationale discusses the gap.

metrics.label: Small biz share of carbon revenue

promises.evidence: Fuel charge rate set to zero effective April 1, 2025. Signed by PM on March 14, 2025.
promises.statusSourceLabel: Department of Finance — consumer fuel charge eliminated announcement

promises.evidence: OBPS maintained and extended to 2035 under Made-in-Canada Competitiveness Strategy.
Concept discussed at Davos. No legislation or framework published.

perspectives.critics: Canadian Climate Institute: industrial credits trade at ~$20/tonne despite a $95 headline price — the "preserved" system is largely performative. NDP: eliminating the consumer rebate hurt low-income households who received more in rebates than they paid in carbon costs.
perspectives.defenders: The consumer tax had become a political liability undermining public support for any climate action. Preserving the industrial system maintains the investment signal for large emitters while removing household burden. Politically and economically defensible.

nextTrigger: OBPS tightening schedule or new effective-price evidence. Carbon border adjustment proposal. Broader replacement climate-plan changes remain primary to Climate & Environment.
inherited: Consumer fuel charge at $80/tonne, on track for $170 by 2030. Industrial OBPS operating alongside. Both politically and economically contentious.

name: Climate & Environment
whatThisGrades: Federal climate and environmental policy — emissions path, conservation, and whether targets hold.
status: Major environmental policy reversals. No replacement strategy. CCI confirms Canada on track for half of required 2030 reduction. See Carbon Pricing Policy for industrial pricing details.
construct: The scale and coherence of the federal environmental policy framework.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: Major environmental reversals occurred without a credible replacement strategy, targets are acknowledged as out of reach, and institutional capacity is shrinking.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: Current D reflects large policy reversals without a funded replacement framework, declining ECCC capacity, and an openly deteriorating path to the 2030 target.

activeModifiers.name: External Constraint
activeModifiers.reason: The trade war may justify some tactical reversals, but it does not justify the full scale of environmental rollbacks reflected in the current grade.

up: Replacement climate strategy published with funded measures
ECCC budget restored

rationale: The Canadian Climate Institute’s Feb 2026 assessment found Canada on track for 18-22% emissions reduction by 2030, against a 40-45% target, and IISD analysis likewise argues that current policy remains insufficient to close the target gap. The PM acknowledged targets will be missed. ECCC faces cumulative budget reductions of approximately $1.3B by 2030 and a workforce reduction of roughly 840 full-time positions (~10% of staff), per departmental plans and media reporting. The emissions cap, EV mandate, Greener Homes Grant, and tree-planting program were eliminated or halved. The trade-war context provides partial justification, but the scale goes beyond tactical adjustment. The gas tax suspension (April 14, $2.4B cost) adds further pressure.

sources.label: ECCC 2024-25 Departmental plan at a glance
ECCC 2025-26 Departmental plan at a glance
CBC — ECCC job and budget cuts

metrics.label: Emissions cap
metrics.sourceNote: Emissions cap effectively suspended per PM acknowledgment, CCI's off-course assessment, and IISD's analysis that stronger policy is still required to meet the 2030 target. Source URLs in the dimension sources array.

metrics.label: EV mandate
metrics.sourceNote: Zero-emission vehicle mandate repealed November 2025. Event-driven policy reversal documented by CCI and media.

metrics.label: ECCC budget cuts (to 2030)
metrics.sourceNote: ECCC departmental plan pages document refocusing reductions of $43M (2024-25), $63M (2025-26), and $91M annually from 2026-27 onward, implying roughly $1.3B in cumulative reductions by 2030. The staffing pressure is documented through departmental plan staffing tables and contemporaneous media reporting on the associated cuts. See the ECCC departmental-plan links in the dimension sources, plus CBC Radio and The Conversation for operational impact reporting.

promises.evidence: Concept announced. No construction timeline or funding structure confirmed.
$3.8B recommitted for 30x30 target (March 2026). Marine target will fall short at 28%.
PM acknowledged 2030 and 2035 targets will be missed. No revised pathway published.

PM publicly acknowledged to Radio-Canada that Canada will miss both targets. CCI projects roughly half of required reduction. Targets not formally withdrawn but acknowledged as unachievable under current policy.

perspectives.critics: Canadian Climate Institute: Canada is on track for half the required 2030 emissions reduction. PM himself acknowledged targets will be missed. ECCC losing 840+ positions. No replacement strategy exists. National Observer: "the fight against climate crisis has disappeared from Ottawa's radar."
perspectives.defenders: The trade-war context is real — maintaining the full climate framework during tariff escalation would have compounded economic pressure. The 30x30 conservation commitment ($3.8B) is genuine. Industrial carbon pricing is preserved. 79% of electricity is already low-carbon.

nextTrigger: Government response to CCI February 2026 assessment. Any new climate plan or replacement strategy. ECCC budget execution. OBPS-only pricing changes remain primary to Carbon Pricing Policy.
inherited: Broad climate policy framework inherited (emissions cap, EV mandate, carbon pricing, conservation), but already off-track on 2030 targets. Trade war creating genuine economic pressure.

name: Immigration
whatThisGrades: Federal immigration policy — permanent resident levels, temporary residents, and student/worker streams.
status: Sharp immigration contraction. Correction is substantial and necessary. Long-term model not yet defined (tracked as next grade trigger).
construct: The adequacy and coherence of the federal immigration level correction.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: The correction has been executed sharply and at real scale, but the long-term model is not yet defined and downside risks remain only partly addressed.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: Current C+ reflects a substantial correction that addresses a real overload problem while still leaving the long-term design unresolved and exposing service-sensitive sectors to downside risk.

up: Government publishes a right-sized long-term model
Temporary residents reach the 5% target ahead of schedule
down: Evidence of critical service failure directly attributable to the pullback

rationale: The government executed a sharp immigration contraction — the largest reduction in temporary resident intake in recent decades (IRCC data: -53% in new arrivals, Jan-Sep 2025 vs 2024). This addresses a real absorption failure — housing, healthcare, and labour market capacity were overwhelmed. However, the correction creates downside risks (healthcare staffing, agriculture, higher ed). The right-sized long-term model has not been defined, which is tracked as the primary trigger for the next grade movement rather than a current grading input. The Toronto Star frames this as cautious stabilization rather than bold reform.

metrics.label: PR target (2026-2028)
metrics.sourceNote: IRCC 2026-2028 Immigration Levels Plan (November 2025): 380,000 permanent residents annually for 2026-2028, range 350,000-420,000. Primary IRCC URL (canada.ca/en/immigration-refugees-citizenship/corporate/mandate/corporate-initiatives/levels/supplementary-immigration-levels-2026-2028.html) returned 403 during verification. Figure independently confirmed by PBO 'Demographic Implications of the 2026-2028 Immigration Levels Plan' (RP-2526-025-S, pbo-dpb.ca), EY immigration analysis, and Clark Hill immigration law review — all citing 380,000.

metrics.label: Temp arrivals change (Jan-Sep 2025 vs 2024)
metrics.sourceNote: IRCC data via media reporting (Dec 2025): 53% decline in new international students and temporary workers, Jan-Sep 2025 vs same period 2024. IRCC open data portal: open.canada.ca/data/en/dataset/360024f2 (work permits) and /90115b00 (study permits). Note: IRCC open data shows 55% work permit decline and 76% study permit decline for Jan-Jun 2025 vs Jan-Jun 2024 — the 53% figure covers a broader population (students + workers combined) over a longer period (Jan-Sep).

metrics.label: Student permits change (Jan-Sep 2025 vs 2024)
metrics.sourceNote: IRCC data via media reporting (Dec 2025): 60% decline in new international student arrivals, Jan-Sep 2025 vs same period 2024 (150,220 fewer students). IRCC open data portal: open.canada.ca/data/en/dataset/90115b00. Note: IRCC open data shows 76% decline for Jan-Jun period specifically; the 60% figure covers the longer Jan-Sep window.

promises.evidence: 380,000 PR target for 2026-2028 (per IRCC levels plan, November 2025). IRCC source page could not be directly verified in baseline pass — figure widely reported but primary URL returned 403.

promises.text: Temp residents below 5% by end of 2027
promises.evidence: Non-permanent residents dropped from 3.15M (7.6%) to 2.68M. On track but not yet at 5%.
Student permits down 60%. TFW program tightened. Asylum stream reforms pending.

perspectives.critics: Toronto Star and healthcare groups: the sharp pullback creates real staffing shortfalls in hospitals, long-term care, and agriculture. Higher education faces revenue collapse. NDP: temporary workers were exploited under the old system — the fix should protect workers, not just cut numbers.
perspectives.defenders: The Bank of Canada found the surge suppressed wages by 0.7% and widened the wage gap to -22.6%. Housing and healthcare were overwhelmed. Even the Fraser Institute acknowledges the correction was necessary. StatsCan reports population declined by 102,436 in 2025 (preliminary) — the pressure valve is working.

nextTrigger: Next quarterly population estimate (StatsCan). IRCC monthly data. Healthcare staffing data showing pullback impact.
inherited: Temporary residents peaked at 3.15M (7.6% of population). Housing, healthcare, and labour absorption overwhelmed. Wage suppression documented by Bank of Canada.

name: Housing Supply
whatThisGrades: Whether federal housing policy is delivering homes at the scale Canada needs.
status: 4,000 BCH units announced, but construction is not yet underway. Ontario deal is real but scale insufficient.
construct: The scale and effectiveness of federal housing policy interventions relative to identified need.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: Starts remain below the needed level, federal programs cover only a tiny share of the shortfall, and spending is declining rather than scaling.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: Current D reflects the gap between announced scale and actual starts, the absence of live Build Canada Homes construction, and a federal spending path that is shrinking rather than accelerating.

activeModifiers.name: Jurisdictional limits
activeModifiers.reason: A C+ cap could matter when more than half the result depends on non-federal actors without agreements, but the current D sits below that cap.

activeModifiers.name: Timing Fairness
activeModifiers.reason: Construction is multi-year, so direction of travel for the BCH pipeline matters, but announcements still do not count as starts.

up: Build Canada Homes construction actually begins and starts exceed 300K annualized
down: Federal spending declines further without offset

rationale: Housing delivery remains a fraction of identified need. Build Canada Homes announced 4,000 units (construction not yet underway) against a shortfall of hundreds of thousands. The Ontario partnership ($8.8B) targets a real barrier (development charges) but is spread over a decade. Federal housing spending declines 56% by 2028-29 — from $9.8B to $4.3B (PBO, December 2025). This is leaning toward a proved failure, not merely too early to judge. Policy Options notes supply alone won’t solve the structural affordability problem.

metrics.label: CMHC annual need
metrics.sourceNote: CMHC 'Canada's Housing Supply Shortages' series: 3.45 million additional units needed. Annual construction need: 430,000-480,000 units by 2035 to restore affordability. See cmhc-schl.gc.ca housing supply shortages reports.

metrics.label: BCH units (announced, not started, 6 federal sites)
metrics.sourceNote: Housing Infrastructure Canada: 'Build Canada Homes aims to build 4,000 factory-built units in six select Canadian cities' (Dartmouth, Longueuil, Ottawa, Toronto, Winnipeg, Edmonton). Source: canada.ca/en/housing-infrastructure-communities/news/2026/01/build-canada-homes-thousands-of-homes-in-the-pipeline.html. Construction expected to begin 2026. PBO independently projects BCH delivers about 26,000 units total over 5 years (RP-2526-020-S).

metrics.label: Federal spending trajectory
metrics.sourceNote: PBO report RP-2526-020-S (December 2025): federal planned spending on housing programs declines from $9.8B in 2025-26 to $4.3B in 2028-29 = 56% decline. Due to expiry of existing program funding and Budget 2025 cuts.

promises.evidence: 259,028 starts in 2025 vs. 450,000+ needed. PBO projects BCH delivers about 26,000 units over five years (a 2.1% increase in housing completions per PBO baseline).
4,000 units announced across 6 sites. Construction not yet underway as of April 2026.
$8.8B over 10 years. Development charges cut up to 50% for 3 years. HST removed on homes under $1M for 1 year. Expert Mike Moffatt called it a meaningful step.
promises.statusSourceLabel: Government of Canada — Canada-Ontario Housing Partnership announcement

promises.evidence: Program announced in Budget 2025. Enrollment data not yet available.

perspectives.critics: NDP: where is the public housing? PBO projects Build Canada Homes delivers about 26,000 units over five years — a 2.1% increase in completions. Federal spending declines 56% by 2028-29 (PBO). The Tyee: Vancouver increased housing stock 200% while prices soared — supply alone does not deliver affordability. This is a proved failure, not "too early to judge."
perspectives.defenders: The Ontario partnership targets a real barrier (development charges) with real money ($8.8B). Expert Mike Moffatt called it meaningful. Housing is a shared-jurisdiction problem — the federal government cannot unilaterally override municipal zoning or provincial building codes.

nextTrigger: Next CMHC monthly starts release. Actual Build Canada Homes construction commencement. Ontario partnership first results.
inherited: 430-480K annual starts needed, ~245K actual. Construction insolvencies highest of any sector. Development charges up 1,000% in some municipalities.

name: Ethics & Transparency
whatThisGrades: Whether the PM's ethics arrangements match the unusual scope of his business background.
status: PM ethics framework incomplete relative to novel background. No proven wrongdoing. Disclosure gap unresolved.
construct: The adequacy of the PM's ethics framework relative to the novel disclosure requirements of his background.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: Disclosure is partial, an ethics screen exists, but completeness is questioned and no independent review has been published.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: C sits at the lower edge of the C band — the framework exists and produces baseline disclosure (distinguishing it from D), but Democracy Watch-level critique and an unpublished Ethics Commissioner review prevent progression toward B.

up: Ethics Commissioner publishes a detailed review finding adequate disclosure
PM proactively publishes full Brookfield accounting
down: New credible governance critique at the Democracy Watch level

rationale: No public evidence demonstrates self-dealing. This dimension grades the adequacy of the PM’s ethics framework relative to the novel disclosure requirements of his background. The framework is assessed as a single construct encompassing disclosure completeness, conflict screening, and independent review — not as separate items. Carney held ~$6.8M USD in unexercised Brookfield options. The Ethics Commissioner has not published a detailed review. Democracy Watch describes the ethics screen as “loophole-filled.” The disclosure standard for a PM with this background should include full public accounting reviewed independently.

sources.label: Office of the Ethics Commissioner — registry and reviews
PM Annex A public declaration of agreed measure

metrics.label: Ethics Commissioner review
metrics.sourceNote: No PM-specific Ethics Commissioner review is published in the Office's current public materials.

metrics.label: Blind trust status
metrics.sourceNote: PM blind-trust summary statement records divestment of listed assets into a blind trust established pursuant to section 27 of the Act.

metrics.label: Agreed measure filing
metrics.sourceNote: Annex A public declaration of agreed measure is published.

metrics.label: Conflict screening scope
metrics.sourceNote: Annex A publicly lists the entities covered by the agreed measure and ethics screen.

metrics.label: Independent governance review
metrics.sourceNote: No non-Commissioner independent governance review is published in the current public materials cited for this dimension.

promises.evidence: Ethics screen and blind trust established. Democracy Watch and House Ethics Committee have raised concerns about completeness.
No published independent review of the structural conflict between career path and policy decisions.
promises.statusSourceLabel: Office of the Ethics Commissioner — registry

perspectives.critics: Democracy Watch: the ethics screen is "loophole-filled." Carney held ~$6.8M in unexercised Brookfield options. The structural alignment between his career (TCFD, GFANZ, Brookfield) and preserving industrial carbon pricing is the definition of a perception conflict. Why has this not been resolved proactively?
perspectives.defenders: No public evidence demonstrates self-dealing. The frameworks Carney helped build (carbon pricing, ESG disclosure) are mainstream G7 consensus, not one individual's agenda. The industrial carbon system would likely exist under any PM. The Ethics Commissioner has not found wrongdoing.

nextTrigger: Ethics Commissioner publication of Brookfield review. Any new disclosure of retained financial interests. House Ethics Committee report.
inherited: No prior PM had Carney's specific background in carbon market architecture and transition finance. Novel disclosure challenge without clear precedent.

name: Flagship Delivery
whatThisGrades: How well the government is executing on its five flagship files — defence, housing, major projects, climate, immigration.
status: Strong on defence. Weak on housing and climate.
construct: The federal government's cross-cutting capacity to convert announcements into measurable results across its five highest-profile policy files.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: Per the Combination Rule, the headline grade is determined mechanically by the delivery-status distribution across the flagship files.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: C sits at the lower edge of the C band — the delivery machinery produces real execution on defence and partial execution on immigration (distinguishing it from D), but stalled housing, major projects, and climate files prevent progression toward B.

up: Per Combination Rule, at least one flagship file improves by one status category
down: Per Combination Rule, at least one flagship file worsens by one status category

rationale: This dimension measures the government's cross-cutting capacity to convert announcements into measurable results across its five highest-profile files: defence (NATO 2%), housing (Build Canada Homes), major projects (MPO), climate (policy framework), and immigration (levels reduction). It is deliberately narrow — it grades execution machinery, not policy merit. Defence shows the government can deliver when focused. Housing and climate show it cannot yet deliver at scale on domestic files. The 40,000 public service job cuts while pursuing an ambitious agenda raise structural capacity questions, a concern reinforced by C.D. Howe's analysis of a worsening executive-to-rank-and-file ratio.

metrics.label: Flagship files delivering
metrics.sourceNote: Editorial assessment per Flagship-Delivery-Rules.md Combination Rule. Status derived from home dimension evidence, not an independent metric.

metrics.label: Flagship files stalled
metrics.sourceNote: Editorial assessment per Flagship-Delivery-Rules.md Combination Rule. Status derived from home dimension evidence, not an independent metric.

metrics.label: Federal-provincial coordination
metrics.sourceNote: Policy Options / IRPP analysis of competitive federalism failures. Source URL in dimension sources array. Editorial characterization, not a quantitative metric.

promises.evidence: National interest designation unused. Most referred projects were already in motion. Federalism coordination gaps documented by Policy Options.
promises.originalSourceLabel: Bill C-5 — One Canadian Economy Act on LEGISinfo

perspectives.critics: Policy Options: cooperative federalism is failing — BC's Premier was blindsided by a pipeline deal, Ontario got hours of notice. NDP and PSAC: you cannot cut 40,000 public service positions while pursuing the most ambitious delivery agenda in years; C.D. Howe notes Budget 2025 also worsens the public-service executive-to-rank-and-file ratio. The state literally cannot execute what the government announces.
perspectives.defenders: Defence and trade show genuine follow-through — NATO 2%, trade diversification, One Canadian Economy Act. The government has demonstrated it can execute when focused. 11 of 26 bills passed in 13 months under minority conditions. The majority secured April 13 should accelerate the domestic agenda.

nextTrigger: Post-majority legislative pace (bills passed per month). First Ministers Meeting outcomes. April 28 economic update implementation.
inherited: Federal delivery capacity had been declining. Procurement timelines among slowest in OECD. Multiple program delivery failures documented in prior government.

name: Promise Delivery
whatThisGrades: A running tracker of specific government commitments — delivered, in progress, stalled, or abandoned.
status: Standalone accountability tracker, kept separate from the 11 performance grades. Carney government has delivered on defence, trade, and key reversals. Housing and climate commitments fall short.
construct: The cumulative rate of fulfillment of specific stated government commitments.

gradeBasis.bandCriterion: Delivery is mixed: some commitments are genuinely fulfilled, while others remain stalled or abandoned and the pattern is not independently clean of the home dimensions.
gradeBasis.plusMinusRationale: Current C+ reflects genuine delivery on several high-profile commitments, but persistent underdelivery on housing and climate and the derivative nature of the dimension keep it from reading as a stronger independent score.

activeModifiers.name: Timing Fairness
activeModifiers.reason: Many commitments are multi-year, so a 'Too Early' category remains necessary when judging the cumulative delivery pattern.

up: Delivery count rises to 18 or more of 43 without new abandonments
down: Additional abandonments bring the total above 15
Housing and climate see no movement for another cycle

rationale: The Carney government made specific commitments on defence (NATO 2%), trade diversification, carbon tax elimination, immigration reduction, and interprovincial reform — and delivered on all of them. These are genuine achievements. However, housing targets have been scaled back, climate commitments were largely reversed rather than delivered, and the pattern across domestic files is consistent: ambitious announcements followed by results that fall short. The grade reflects real delivery on priority files alongside persistent underdelivery on household-impact files. Note: Promise Delivery is a derivative dimension. Grade movements that are already captured in their home dimensions (e.g., housing delivery moving Housing Supply) should not independently move this grade. This dimension tracks the cumulative pattern of commitment fulfillment as a political accountability measure, not as an independent policy performance indicator.

sources.label: Angus Reid — Carney government report card

metrics.label: Key commitments delivered
metrics.sourceNote: Editorial tally based on promise-by-promise status assessment. Each promise status is documented with evidence in the promises array. Derivative of home dimension evidence.

metrics.label: Commitments stalled or abandoned
metrics.sourceNote: Editorial tally. See individual promise entries for evidence basis.

metrics.label: Housing target
metrics.sourceNote: Editorial characterization based on Housing Supply dimension evidence. Derivative of home dimension assessment.

metrics.label: Climate commitments
metrics.sourceNote: Editorial characterization based on Climate & Environment dimension evidence. Derivative of home dimension assessment.

promises.evidence: Day 1 commitment. Fuel charge set to zero April 1, 2025.
promises.statusSourceLabel: Department of Finance — consumer fuel charge eliminated announcement

promises.evidence: Reversed in Budget 2025. Removed disinvestment incentive.
promises.statusSourceLabel: Department of Finance — capital gains inclusion rate announcement

promises.evidence: PR targets cut to 380,000. Temp arrivals down 53% (Jan-Sep 2025 vs 2024). Population declined by 102,436 in 2025 (StatsCan, preliminary).
One Canadian Economy Act passed with bipartisan support. Genuine structural reform.
promises.statusSourceLabel: Government of Canada — Internal Trade Action Plan status

promises.evidence: Effectively suspended. Guilbeault resigned from cabinet over the pipeline MOU.
promises.originalSourceLabel: ECCC proposed oil & gas emissions cap framework (Nov 2024)
promises.statusSourceLabel: ECCC proposed oil & gas emissions cap framework

promises.evidence: Repealed. Limited connection to trade competitiveness rationale.
Program halved. Tangential connection to trade-war justification.

perspectives.critics: NDP and environmental groups: the climate rollbacks (emissions cap, EV mandate, tree planting) are abandoned commitments dressed as pragmatism. Several have no connection to trade competitiveness. Housing targets have been quietly scaled back. Rhetoric consistently outpaces results.
perspectives.defenders: Fraser Institute acknowledges the consumer carbon tax elimination, capital gains reversal, immigration correction, and interprovincial reform as genuine steps in the right direction. Defence delivery is real and verified by NATO. About half the reversals are supported across the political spectrum.

nextTrigger: Next major policy announcement or delivery milestone. Housing starts trajectory. Climate replacement strategy if any.
inherited: 15 major Trudeau-era policies inherited, several already controversial. Election framed as sovereignty referendum, creating specific mandate expectations.
